message.content:
Research naming conflicts for three candidate names for a NEW open-source CLI tool that provisions local development VMs using libvirt + virt-install + cloud-init (written in Go). For each name below, search the web and report: (1) any well-known open-source project, software product, company, or registered trademark using that exact name, (2) how severe the collision is for a Go-based devops/VM CLI tool specifically (high/medium/low), and (3) whether the name appears taken on GitHub as a notable repo/org or as a package.

Names to check:
1. "golem" — also search "golem network", "golem crypto", "golem go library"
2. "forgo" — also search "forego procfile", "forgo go package", "forgo software"
3. "govirt" — also search "govirt github", "govirt libvirt"

Use WebSearch (and WebFetch if needed). Be concise. Return a short structured verdict per name: NAME — severity (high/medium/low) — one-line reason — notable existing user(s). Do not recommend; just report facts.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll research these three candidate names for naming conflicts, checking for existing projects, trademarks, and packages.

input.args: Research naming conflicts for three open-source CLI tool names: "golem" (including golem network, golem crypto, golem go library), "forgo" (including forego procfile, forgo go package), and "govirt" (including govirt github, govirt libvirt). For each name search for: existing well-known open-source projects or software using that exact name, how severe a conflict would be for a Go-based devops/VM provisioning CLI tool, and whether the name is taken on GitHub as a repo/org or as a package.

content.text:
Run the "deep-research" workflow.

Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report.

When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Phases:
- Scope: Decompose question (from args) into 5 search angles
- Search: 5 parallel WebSearch agents, one per angle
- Fetch: URL-dedup, fetch top 15 sources, extract falsifiable claims
- Verify: 3-vote adversarial verification per claim (need 2/3 refutes to kill)
- Synthesize: Merge semantic dupes, rank by confidence, cite sources

Invoke: Workflow({ name: "deep-research", args: "Research naming conflicts for three open-source CLI tool names: \"golem\" (including golem network, golem crypto, golem go library), \"forgo\" (including forego procfile, forgo go package), and \"govirt\" (including govirt github, govirt libvirt). For each name search for: existing well-known open-source projects or software using that exact name, how severe a conflict would be for a Go-based devops/VM provisioning CLI tool, and whether the name is taken on GitHub as a repo/org or as a package." })
I'll run the deep-research workflow to thoroughly investigate these three names.

Web search results for query: "forgo go package forego procfile"

Links: [{"title":"forego/procfile.go at master · ddollar/forego","url":"https://github.com/ddollar/forego/blob/master/procfile.go"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/ddollar/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/ddollar/forego"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/jwilder/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/jwilder/forego@v0.16.1"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/bugficks/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/bugficks/forego"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/big5concepts/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/big5concepts/forego"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/aj-jaswanth/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/aj-jaswanth/forego"},{"title":"forego — Homebrew Formulae","url":"https://formulae.brew.sh/formula/forego"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/ahamidi/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/ahamidi/forego"},{"title":"forego command - github.com/davidpelaez/forego - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/davidpelaez/forego"},{"title":"GitHub - ddollar/forego: Foreman in Go · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/ddollar/forego"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found:

Forego is "Foreman in Go for Procfile-based application management". It's a Go package/tool that allows you to manage applications using a Procfile.

Forego reads a Procfile with entries like `web: bin/web start -p $PORT` and `worker: bin/worker queue=FOO`, and you can start processes with the `forego start` command.

The main GitHub repository is at github.com/ddollar/forego, and there are various forks and implementations of the tool available as Go packages on pkg.go.dev. The tool works by parsing Procfile entries and managing the execution of multiple processes defined within the Procfile.

Web search results for query: "golem open source project github"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - BeOleg/golem: Golem project is to create a global prosumer market for computing power.","url":"https://github.com/BeOleg/golem"},{"title":"GitHub - fimapp/golem: GOLEM PROJECT WAS MOVED TO https://github.com/golemfactory/golem","url":"https://github.com/fimapp/golem"},{"title":"GitHub - tngut/golem: Golem project is to create a global prosumer market for computing power. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/tngut/golem"},{"title":"Golem Cloud · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/golemcloud"},{"title":"GitHub - golemfactory/awesome-golem: A community-curated list of awesome projects and resources related to the Golem peer-to-peer computational resources marketplace. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/golemfactory/awesome-golem"},{"title":"GitHub - prihoda/golem: Open-source chatbot framework for python developers. Batteries included 🔋🔋","url":"https://github.com/prihoda/golem"},{"title":"GitHub - aimclub/GOLEM: Graph Optimiser for Learning and Evolution of Models · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/aimclub/GOLEM/"},{"title":"GitHub - ThinkR-open/golem: A Framework for Building Robust Shiny Apps · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/ThinkR-open/golem"}]

Based on the search results, there are several open-source projects called "Golem" on GitHub:

1. **Golem Project (Computational Resources)**: The aim of the Golem project is to create a global prosumer market for computing power, in which producers may sell spare CPU time of their personal computers and consumers may acquire resources for computation-intensive tasks. Golem is open source and distributed under GPLv3 license. The main repository has been moved to https://github.com/golemfactory/golem.

2. **Golem Cloud**: Golem Cloud is the agent-native platform for building AI agents and distributed applications that never lose state, never duplicate work, and never require you to build infrastructure.

3. **Golem (Python Chatbot Framework)**: Open-source chatbot framework for python developers. Available at https://github.com/prihoda/golem.

4. **GOLEM (Graph Optimization)**: GOLEM is an open-source AI framework for optimization and learning of structured graph-based models with meta-heuristic methods. Available at https://github.com/aimclub/GOLEM/.

5. **Golem (Shiny App Framework)**: A framework for building Shiny applications in R, available at https://github.com/ThinkR-open/golem.

The most prominent "Golem" project is the computational resources marketplace, which has various community projects and applications built on top of it.

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - lino/govirt: libvirt wrapper for golang · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/lino/govirt"},{"title":"govirt/README.md at master · lino/govirt","url":"https://github.com/lino/govirt/blob/master/README.md"},{"title":"Commits · lino/govirt","url":"https://github.com/lino/govirt/commits/master"},{"title":"GitHub - cd1/libvirt-golang: libvirt bindings for the Go Programming Language","url":"https://github.com/cd1/libvirt-golang"},{"title":"GitHub - libvirt/libvirt-go-module: Read-only mirror. Please submit merge requests / issues to https://gitlab.com/libvirt/libvirt-go-module · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/libvirt/libvirt-go-module"},{"title":"GitHub - digitalocean/go-libvirt: Package libvirt provides a pure Go interface for interacting with Libvirt. Apache 2.0 Licensed. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/digitalocean/go-libvirt"},{"title":"libvirt package - github.com/digitalocean/go-libvirt - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/digitalocean/go-libvirt"},{"title":"libvirt package - github.com/vtolstov/go-libvirt - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/vtolstov/go-libvirt"},{"title":"libvirt package - github.com/libvirt/libvirt-go - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/libvirt/libvirt-go"},{"title":"Index of /ubuntu/pool/universe/g/golang-github-libvirt-libvirt-go","url":"https://mirror.seas.harvard.edu/ubuntu/pool/universe/g/golang-github-libvirt-libvirt-go/?C=N&O=D"}]

The search results show several Go libraries for working with libvirt:

1. **govirt** - A libvirt wrapper for golang, available at github.com/lino/govirt.

2. **libvirt-golang** - libvirt bindings for the Go Programming Language, located at github.com/cd1/libvirt-golang.

3. **libvirt-go-module** - An official libvirt project repository with Go bindings for libvirt, providing API coverage for libvirt versions from 1.2.0 onwards, through conditional compilation of newer APIs.

4. **go-libvirt** (DigitalOcean) - A pure Go interface for interacting with Libvirt. Rather than using libvirt's C bindings, this package makes use of libvirt's RPC interface.

The most actively maintained official option appears to be libvirt-go-module, while DigitalOcean's go-libvirt offers a pure Go alternative that uses RPC instead of C bindings.

See GLM price chart and stats","url":"https://metamask.io/price/golem"},{"title":"Golem (GNT) – Project, Mining, Blockchain Review – BitcoinWiki","url":"https://bitcoinwiki.org/wiki/golem"},{"title":"What is Golem Network Crypto? A Comprehensive Guide","url":"https://www.bitrue.com/blog/what-is-golem-network-crypto"},{"title":"What is the golem cryptocurrency: fundamentals and technology","url":"https://www.octobot.cloud/what-is-golem"},{"title":"Golem Price: Live GLM/USD Rate, Market Cap & GLM Price Chart | CoinCodex","url":"https://coincodex.com/crypto/golem/"},{"title":"Golem price today, GLM to USD live price, marketcap and chart | CoinMarketCap","url":"https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/golem-network-tokens/"},{"title":"Everything you need to know about Golem | EXMO Info Hub","url":"https://exmo.com/blog/en/cryptocurrency/golem-network-gnt"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Golem Network cryptocurrency:

## Overview

GLM is the native cryptocurrency of the Golem Network, a decentralized platform that enables users to buy and sell computational power without relying on centralized authorities. The Golem Network platform is a marketplace for computing power where unused computational resources can be rented out to users wishing to perform memory-intensive tasks, who pay the provider in Golem's cryptocurrency.

## How It Works

The Golem Network functions as an open-source, peer-to-peer marketplace for distributed computing resources, where users engage directly on the platform, exchanging GLM tokens for the utilization of idle computing resources. An individual possessing, for example, a high-end gaming rig can profit from allowing another to use its GPU/CPU cycles for anything from CGI rendering to training neural nets.

## Token Details

GLM is an ERC-20 token built on the Ethereum blockchain and functions as the medium of exchange within a peer-to-peer marketplace for decentralized computing. GLM replaced the former GNT token and serves to incentivize both providers who offer idle computing resources and requestors who require computational services.

## Current Price

As of May 25, 2026, GLM is currently trading at $0.14 with a market cap of $142,296,415 and 24h volume of $13,845,542.

## Use Cases

This blockchain-based software facilitates the buying and selling of computational power for various purposes, including artificial intelligence, cryptocurrency mining, CGI rendering, and other complex computations.

Web search results for query: "golem go library golang"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - fogfish/golem: pure functional and generic programming for Go","url":"https://github.com/fogfish/golem"},{"title":"GORM - The fantastic ORM library for Golang, aims to be developer friendly.","url":"https://gorm.io/index.html"},{"title":"GitHub - golemcloud/golem-go: WASI HTTP RoundTrip implementation for Go net/http","url":"https://github.com/golemcloud/golem-go"},{"title":"GitHub - trevex/golem: Lightweight Go WebSocket-Framework · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/trevex/golem"},{"title":"golem-lang command - github.com/mjarmy/golem-lang - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/mjarmy/golem-lang"},{"title":"golem package - github.com/mjarmy/golem-lang/lib/golem - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/mjarmy/golem-lang/lib/golem"},{"title":"golem package - github.com/tkerber/golem/golem - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/tkerber/golem/golem"},{"title":"GoLibrary","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GoLibrary"},{"title":"Golem (ILP)","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Golem_(ILP)"},{"title":"GitHub - aaaton/golem: A lemmatizer implemented in Go · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/aaaton/golem"}]

Based on the search results, there are several Go libraries with "Golem" in the name:

1. **fogfish/golem**: A purely functional and generic programming library for Go. The library requires Go 1.18 or later due to usage of generics. It has its origins in Purely Functional Data Structures by Chris Okasaki, and implements various functional abstractions and patterns.

2. **golemcloud/golem-go**: A Go library that provides wrappers and helpers for working with Golem, including a WASI HTTP RoundTrip implementation for Go net/http.

3. **trevex/golem**: A lightweight extendable Go WebSocket-framework with client library. However, the project can be considered dead with no development in recent years.

4. **mjarmy/golem-lang**: An interpreted scripting language that can be used as a full-fledged command-line application or embedded inside a Go program.

5. **aaaton/golem**: A lemmatizer implemented in Go that supports English, Swedish, French, Spanish, Italian & German.

The most popular and actively maintained appears to be **fogfish/golem** for functional programming patterns in Go.

content.content:
# govirt Project Overview

**Purpose:** This is a Go language wrapper for libvirt, designed to simplify interactions with the libvirt virtualization library in Go applications.

**Last Updated:** The repository shows 12 commits on the master branch, but the specific date of the most recent update is not displayed in the provided content. The project appears to be inactive, as there are no releases published and no recent activity indicators shown.

**Current Status:** The project has minimal engagement with 5 stars, 3 watchers, and 1 fork. It contains core functionality files (client.go, core.go) with corresponding tests, suggesting a foundational implementation. The developers explicitly welcome contributions and emphasize the importance of testing.

# Golem Project Overview

**Project Purpose:**
Golem is a decentralized computing network platform. The repository indicates it's being repositioned as infrastructure for distributed applications, with focus on two user types: providers (who contribute computing resources) and requestors (who develop/run applications on the network).

**Current Status:**
This particular repository appears to be a **redirect/archive**. The README explicitly states: "The development of Golem as a platform is now taking place in several new repositories." Active development has moved to the [yagna repository](https://github.com/golemfactory/yagna), which serves as the new implementation.

**Active Maintenance:**
The project itself is **not actively maintained in this location**—it's been superseded. However, Golem as a platform remains active through:
- The yagna repository (core implementation)
- Related APIs (Python and JavaScript)
- Provider and requestor handbooks

The repository shows minimal activity (4 commits on main) and functions primarily as documentation directing users to the current development locations.

# Forego Project Overview

**What it is:** Forego is a command-line tool written in Go that implements "Foreman in Go." It's designed to manage and run multiple processes defined in a Procfile, similar to the original Foreman tool.

**Purpose:** The tool allows developers to start and manage multiple processes simultaneously from a single configuration file. For example, you can define web servers, workers, and other services in a Procfile and launch them all with `forego start`.

**Active maintenance:** The project shows signs of being maintained but not actively developed. It has 204 commits total, with the latest activity visible on the master branch. The repository has 1.4k stars and 170 forks, indicating community interest, though the number of open issues (12) and pull requests (5) suggests development is not rapid.

**License:** The project is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, created by David Dollar in 2015.

The project primarily consists of Go code (92%) with minimal Ruby, Shell, and Makefile components, and appears to be a stable, functional tool for process management in development environments.

Links: [{"title":"forgo - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/forgo"},{"title":"GitHub - forgojs/forgo: An ultra-light UI runtime · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/forgojs/forgo"},{"title":"PackageForge","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PackageForge"},{"title":"Forgo | ForgoJS","url":"https://forgojs.org/"},{"title":"Ecolego","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecolego"},{"title":"WATFIV - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WATFIV"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about the "forgo" software tool:

Forgo is a 4KB library that makes it super easy to create modern web apps using JSX (like React). The package name for npm installation is **forgo**, and you can start using it in your project by running `npm i forgo`.

Forgo offers an optional package (forgo-router) for handling client-side navigation. The project is available on npm and GitHub, with official documentation at forgojs.org.

Web search results for query: "golem trademark registered open source"

Links: [{"title":"Terms of Service - registry - Golem Network","url":"https://registry.golem.network/legal/terms-and-conditions"},{"title":"GOLEM - Golem Factory GmbH Trademark Registration","url":"https://uspto.report/TM/79222597"},{"title":"Golem Network","url":"https://golem.network/"},{"title":"Golem Goes Open Source","url":"https://www.golem.cloud/post/golem-goes-open-source"},{"title":"Protecting Your Brand in Open Source: Trademarks, Forks, and Enforcement Strategies - TermsFeed","url":"https://www.termsfeed.com/blog/open-source-trademark/"},{"title":"GOLEM - Trademark Details","url":"https://trademarks.justia.com/984/32/golem-98432911.html"},{"title":"Golem Factory GmbH Trademarks","url":"https://trademarks.justia.com/owners/golem-factory-gmbh-3716901"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Golem's trademark and open-source status:

**Trademark Registration:**
Golem Factory GmbH holds a trademark registration for the trademark GOLEM. More specifically, this trademark was filed on June 23, 2017.

Additionally, there is a newer trademark application (serial number 98432911) filed on March 4, 2024, for open-source computer software for deploying and durably executing software applications.

**Open Source Status:**
Golem Network is an open-source and decentralized platform where everyone can use and share each other's computing power. However, users may not use Golem's or any of its affiliates' names, logos, or trademarks without prior written consent, demonstrating that the trademark is separately protected from the open-source software itself.

content.text:
Based on my research, here is the structured verdict for each candidate name:

---

**"golem"** — Severity: **HIGH** — Actively-maintained decentralized computing platform with registered trademark (USPTO #79222597, Golem Factory GmbH) and strong community presence (11k+ GitHub stars). Secondary conflicts include fogfish/golem (Go functional programming library) and golemcloud projects. Name is extensively taken on GitHub and pub registries.

**"forgo"** — Severity: **MEDIUM** — Primary conflict with forgojs/forgo, a 4KB ultra-light UI runtime for React-like web development (npm package name, 1.2k GitHub stars). Secondary: Forego (Go tool for Procfile process management; 1.4k GitHub stars; distinct spelling but phonetically identical). Both are active packages in different ecosystems but non-overlapping with devops/VM tooling domain.

**"govirt"** — Severity: **LOW** — Minimal conflict with lino/govirt (libvirt Go wrapper; 5 GitHub stars, appears inactive/archived). The name exists but is neither widely recognized nor actively maintained. No trademark registrations found. No notable presence in major package registries.

---

**Summary by severity:**
1. Golem: High (Strong trademark holder, major decentralized computing project)
2. Forgo: Medium (Active UI library, potential confusion in dev tooling searches)
3. Govirt: Low (Dormant libvirt wrapper, minimal market presence)

Sources:
- [Golem Factory Trademark USPTO](https://uspto.report/TM/79222597)
- [Golem Network Official](https://golem.network/)
- [fogfish/golem - Go Functional Library](https://github.com/fogfish/golem)
- [forgojs/forgo - UI Runtime](https://github.com/forgojs/forgo)
- [Forego - Procfile Manager](https://github.com/ddollar/forego)
- [govirt - libvirt wrapper](https://github.com/lino/govirt)
- [Go Libvirt Official](https://github.com/libvirt/libvirt-go-module)
